key_concerns: Pivotal study failed primary endpoint with p=0.97
Serious neurologic adverse events from intrathecal administration
NfL not an established surrogate endpoint
key_positives: Strong target engagement shown by SOD1 reduction
Substantial NfL reduction correlating with disease progression
Severe unmet need for rare SOD1-ALS
FDA regulatory flexibility for accelerated approval

key_concerns: Pivotal study failed primary endpoint with p=0.97
Serious neurologic adverse events from intrathecal administration
NfL not an established surrogate endpoint
key_positives: Strong target engagement shown by SOD1 reduction
Substantial NfL reduction correlating with disease progression
Severe unmet need for rare SOD1-ALS
FDA regulatory flexibility for accelerated approval
evidence_notes: Study failed to show a statistically significant difference for primary endpoint (ALSFRS-R) in fast progressor population (p=0.97)
55% reduction in plasma NfL observed at Week 28 vs 12% increase in placebo (nominal p<0.0001)
Post-hoc analysis suggest a correlation between the reduction in NfL and change from baseline in ALSFRS-R
Causal inference analysis may support the predictive value of plasma NfL
Serious neurologic adverse events including myelitis, radiculitis, and aseptic meningitis

questions_framing.focus: Whether NfL reduction is reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit for accelerated approval

signal.label: MIXED
rationale: Concerns: Pivotal study failed primary endpoint with p=0.97; Statistical uncertainties in post-hoc analyses; Serious neurologic adverse events from intrathecal administration; NfL not an established surrogate endpoint; Confirmatory studies delayed or insufficient

Positives: Strong target engagement shown by SOD1 reduction; Substantial NfL reduction correlating with disease progression; Post-hoc analyses suggest clinical benefit; Severe unmet need for rare SOD1-ALS; FDA regulatory flexibility for accelerated approval
